mnemonics: Every Super Villain Digs Naming Cats. (Explains: Every=Ellipsis, Super=Set, Villain=List, Digs=Dictionary, Naming=NamedTuple, Cats=Class)
keyPoints: Immutable types (int, float, str, tuple) cannot be changed after creation, while mutable types (list, dict, set) can be modified.
Use type() to check an object's data type and isinstance() to check against a type or tuple of types.
Understanding mutability is crucial for avoiding unintended side-effects, especially when passing objects to functions.
commonMistakes: Attempting to modify an immutable type (e.g., trying to change a character in a string or an element in a tuple) which raises an error.
Using a mutable data type (like a list) as a default argument in a function definition, which creates a shared, persistent default value across all function calls.
Confusing the 'is' operator (which checks object identity) with the '==' operator (which checks value equality), especially with small integers that are interned.
